Virginia High School League
Competitive Cheer — Rubric 101 Training
Fall 2026–27 Season · Updated July 13, 2026



▶ Welcome Video
Purpose of This Training
This module is designed to provide a thorough understanding of the 2026–27 VHSL Competitive Cheer scoring rubric. Whether you are a first-year judge or a seasoned coach, this training will ensure you can evaluate routines with confidence, consistency, and accuracy.

By the end of this module, all participants should be able to apply the rubric uniformly — resulting in fair, transparent, and consistent scores at every VHSL competition.
Who Is This Training For?
This training module is intended for:
  • VHSL Competitive Cheer Officials — to ensure consistent and accurate scoring at all levels of competition
  • VHSL Competitive Cheer Head & Assistant Coaches — to understand how routines are evaluated and how to best prepare their teams
Learning Objectives
1
Understand the complete 2026–27 VHSL scoresheet structure and how each category contributes to the final score
2
Identify and apply the five difficulty ranges across Stunts, Pyramids, Tumbling, and Jumps
3
Visually identify and correctly categorize stunt & pyramid skills within the rubric based on video examples
4
Correctly determine majority thresholds based on team size and apply them to difficulty scoring decisions
5
Distinguish between execution categories and apply them accurately
6
Work through real-world scoring scenarios to practice applying the rubric in context before competition season
🧭
How to Navigate This Module: Use the numbered progress bar at the top to move between sections, or use the Previous and Next buttons at the bottom of each page. This module contains 10 sections and can be completed at your own pace. You may revisit any section at any time.
Section 1 of 10
Section 2 of 10
Scoresheet Overview
When evaluating a routine, coaches & judges must understand the complete scoring structure. This module walks through all scoring categories, their point values, and how they combine into a final score.

Total Possible Score

Every routine is scored out of 100 total points per judge, before any technical deductions are applied. The scoresheet is divided into six major categories below.

100points
25pts

Stunts

Difficulty of skills performed by the majority of stunt groups, plus quality of execution.

Difficulty: 10 pts Execution: 15 pts
25pts

Pyramids

Difficulty of pyramid transitions and structure, plus quality of execution.

Difficulty: 10 pts Execution: 15 pts
10pts

Tumbling

Synchronized team sequence difficulty plus technical execution of all tumbling.

Difficulty: 5 pts Execution: 5 pts
10pts

Jumps

Type, variety, and connection of jumps performed by the majority, plus execution.

Difficulty: 5 pts Execution: 5 pts
10pts

Dance + Overall

Entertainment value and execution of the dance section, plus overall choreography & performance.

Entertainment & Execution: 5 pts Overall Choreo: 5 pts
20pts

Cheer

Game-day cheer evaluated in 4 equal categories.

Motion Technique: 5 pts Crowd Effectiveness: 5 pts Prop Incorporation: 5 pts Cheer Impression: 5 pts
⚡
Key Principle: Stunts, pyramids, tumbling, and jumps all have both a Difficulty score and an Execution score. A team can earn high difficulty marks but still lose significant points through poor execution.

Rubric Overview
A detailed overview of how the rubric is structured, the five difficulty ranges, and how scores are calculated across all categories.
▶ Rubric Overview Video
Key Terms & Points of Emphasis
Click on any card below for a full explanation, with pics and videos where available.
Inversions
Understand the difference between the two types of inverted releases.
Learn More →
Stunt Levels
Examine illustrations of extended, prep, and below prep levels.
Learn More →
Twisting
Learn what counts as a legal twisting rotation, and how release twists are credited differently.
Learn More →
Non-Release vs. Release
See the transition types side by side — from maintaining contact to a full release.
Learn More →
Braced vs. Unbraced
Compare braced and unbraced flyer connections and the pyramid contact-timing rule.
Learn More →
Single Base
Learn the requirements a stunt group must meet throughout a skill to qualify as single base.
Learn More →
Section 4 of 10
Section 5 of 10
Majority Breakdown
Understanding majority is essential for every scoring decision. Use the interactive calculator below to quickly find majority thresholds for any team size, then review the key majority rules.

Majority Calculator

Enter the number of athletes performing on the mat to see the required majority numbers for each category.

15


Athletes Stunt Groups
(Single Base or Full Group)		 Pyramid Skill Groups
(Non-Bracer Groups)		 Jumps/Tumbling
203 groups2 skill groups11 athletes
193 groups2 skill groups10 athletes
183 groups2 skill groups10 athletes
173 groups2 skill groups9 athletes
163 groups2 skill groups9 athletes
152 groups1 skill group8 athletes
142 groups1 skill group8 athletes
132 groups1 skill group7 athletes
122 groups1 skill group7 athletes
112 groups1 skill group6 athletes
102 groups1 skill group6 athletes
9 and under2 groups1 skill group5 athletes
Key Majority Rules
Ripples Are Allowed
Ripples (sequential timing of the same exact skill) are allowed within stunt, pyramid, and tumbling sequences but should be clearly defined. It should be very obvious that athletes are not being recycled.
Front Spots & Non-Participation
Front spots (stunt groups of more than 4) or less than full participation from the maximum number of possible stunt groups will decrease the value of a skill within the range.
Pyramid Majority (16+ Athletes)
Teams of 16 or more are required to have at least TWO different flyers (in addition to any bracers) completing the required rubric skills. These may be a connected 2x2 pyramid, two separate mini pyramids, or any combination. Again, ripples are allowed but should be obvious and clearly defined.
If Majority Fails
If a required skill falls before completion, judges look for other transition/lower-level skills. If skills only occur in different ranges without meeting minimums for one range, the score drops to the lowest range executed. If no majority at all → score drops to the 0–1 range.
Section 5 of 10
Section 6 of 10
Difficulty Ranges
The rubric organizes skills into difficulty ranges, each with a point value window. A team's starting range is determined by which skills the majority of groups/athletes successfully execute.
Stunt & Pyramid — Difficulty Ranges
📌
How ranges are determined: Stunts require a minimum of 2 different skills successfully executed within a range (one may be a toss) by the majority of the maximum number of stunt groups. Pyramids require a minimum of 3 different skills within a range by the majority.
Range Point Value
Beginner 0.0 – 1.0
Intermediate 1.25 – 3.25
Advanced 3.5 – 5.5
Elite 5.75 – 7.75
Super Elite 8.0 – 10.0
💡
Increasing difficulty within a range: Performing lower-level skills than the required skill does NOT increase difficulty. However, additional skills within the same assigned range or a higher range CAN increase the score within range because they are of equivalent or higher difficulty. Credit may also be given in Overall Choreography & Performance as "additional skills beyond required minimum."
Jumps — Difficulty Ranges
PointsRequirements
0.0 – 1.5No jumps, no majority, intermediate jumps (tuck/T/spread eagle), or only 1 advanced jump
1.75 – 2.752 advanced jumps with variety (with or without connection)
3.00 – 4.003 unconnected advanced jumps with variety OR 2 connected advanced + 1 unconnected advanced with variety
4.25 – 4.753 connected advanced jumps with variety
5.03 connected advanced jumps with variety performed by the full team
Tumbling — Difficulty Ranges
PointsRequirements (based on synchronized majority sequence)
0.0 – 1.0No tumbling, no majority, back/front walkovers, forward/backward rolls, cartwheels, round offs
1.25 – 2.50Aerials, back handsprings
2.75 – 4.0Tucks
4.25 – 5.0Layouts, fulls
✅
Tumbling Range Credit: Only ONE synchronized team tumbling sequence needs to be performed by the majority to establish range credit. Additional tumbling skills (in any configuration anywhere else in the routine) may increase the score within the range. Jump-to-tumbling sequences may also increase the score within range.
Section 6 of 10
Section 7 of 10
Execution Guidelines
Execution scores are separate from difficulty scores and assess the quality, consistency, and precision with which skills are performed. Stunt/Pyramid execution is evaluated out of 15 points each; Jump/Tumbling execution is evaluated out of 5 points each.
⚡
Important: Execution scores cover all skills performed anywhere in the routine, including during the cheer section.
Category Stunt/Pyramid Score Jump/Tumbling Score Description
Superior 14.0 – 15.0 4.25 – 5.0 Consistent precision with very well-executed skills. Synchronization, spacing, and technique are highly uniform throughout. Minimal minor errors, if any, appear isolated and do not affect overall impression.
Strong 12.0 – 13.75 3.25 – 4.0 Technique is generally clean and controlled with only minor periodic inconsistencies. Timing and spacing remain mostly unified, though some deviations occur. Execution reflects above-average skill completion with room for refinement.
Developing 10.0 – 11.75 2.25 – 3.0 Mixed consistency with notable variation in technique throughout. Timing, spacing, or skill clarity are intermittently misaligned, reducing overall performance impression. Errors appear in multiple sequences/sections.
Inconsistent 8.0 – 9.75 1.25 – 2.0 Many skills performed contained errors or falls. Many timing and/or execution issues. Overall below-average technique with major execution issues throughout the routine.
Needs Significant Improvement 0.0 – 7.75 0.0 – 1.0 Majority of skills show overwhelming execution concerns or lack of completion. Timing, spacing, and technical precision are extremely inconsistent throughout. Performance does not demonstrate foundational technique, repetitive practice with applied corrections, or team cohesion.
Stunt & Pyramid Execution Checklist
✓ Positive ✗ Needs Work
Good Technique Work on Technique
Clean Dismounts Shaky Dismounts
Good Timing & Sync Timing Needs Work
Skills Strong & Confident Falls / Drops / Missed Skills
Jump & Tumbling Execution Checklist
✓ Positive ✗ Needs Work
Good Technique / Form Bent Legs or Arms
Solid / Clean Landings Feet Apart in Landings
Good Timing & Sync Timing Needs Work
Skills Strong & Confident Falls or Missed Skills
Good Height / Flexibility Improve Height / Flexibility

Section 8 of 10
Section 9 of 10
Scoring Scenarios
Work through these real-world scenarios to practice applying the rubric.
Click a card to reveal the scenario and question. Discuss as a group, then click "Show Answer" when ready.
Pyramid
Release Into Single Base
▼
Does this sequence meet the requirement for a single base pyramid? Why or why not?
Show Answer
Stunt
Identify the Inversion
▼
How do we classify this stunt on the rubric, and what range is it scored in?
Show Answer
Pyramid
Unbraced Reconnection
▼
In a pyramid, one flyer performs an unbraced skill, momentarily disconnecting from the flyer beside her. She reconnects to her bracer 3 counts after the skill is completed.
Does this flyer's skill receive pyramid credit?
Show Answer
Jumps
Connected Jump Sequence
▼
The majority of a team performs: Toe Touch → Toe Touch → Pike (3 connected advanced jumps). They have 2 different jumps (Toe Touch and Pike).
What range does this place them in, and would adding a hurdler on the end change anything?
Show Answer
Tumbling
Same Exact Skill in Sync Pass
▼
A team of 18 (tumbling majority = 10 athletes) performs a synchronized sequence where 12 athletes do a round off back handspring then later the same 12 athletes simultaneously do a combination of standing tucks and round off tucks (6 of each). Later in the routine, 2 athletes perform standing fulls.
What tumbling range is established, and can the fulls help the score?
Show Answer
Different Range Scoring Examples
A team successfully executes skills in the ranges shown below. Click each row to reveal the overall difficulty range.
Stunt
Requires 2 successfully executed skills performed by majority.
2 Super Elite
Super Elite
1 Advanced + 1 Elite
Advanced
1 Intermediate + 1 Elite + 1 Super Elite
Elite
1 Advanced + 2 Super Elite
Super Elite
2 Beginner + 1 Intermediate + 1 Advanced
Intermediate
1 Advanced + No Majority Found Elsewhere
Beginner
Pyramid
Requires 3 successfully executed skills performed by majority.
3 Super Elite
Super Elite
1 Intermediate + 1 Elite + 1 Super Elite
Intermediate
1 Advanced + 2 Super Elite
Advanced
2 Beginner + 1 Intermediate + 1 Advanced
Beginner
3 Intermediate + 2 Elite + 1 Super Elite
Elite
2 Super Elite + No Majority Found Elsewhere
Beginner
